1.synthesis: The serpent, “more crafty than any other beast of the field” (v.1), approaches the woman not with a command but with a question that reframes God’s generous prohibition as stinginess: “Did God actually say, ‘You shall not eat of any tree in the garden’?” Eve’s reply both defends and subtly distorts God’s word, adding “neither shall you touch it” (v.3). The serpent then flatly contradicts the threatened death — “You will not surely die” (v.4) — and impugns God’s motive: “you will be like God, knowing good and evil” (v.5). The woman sees the fruit as good, a delight, and desirable to make one wise, takes and eats, and gives to her husband “who was with her” (v.6). Their eyes open — but to shame, not glory — and they sew fig leaves (v.7). The integrative thread is the anatomy of temptation: doubt God’s word, deny his warning, distrust his goodness, and the creature reaches to be its own god.
1.historical_context: The narrative stands in deliberate contrast to the creation-rest of the preceding chapters; the man and woman, made in God’s image and given dominion (Genesis 1:26–28), surrender their rule to a beast they were to govern. The serpent is identified later in Scripture as the ancient deceiver, the devil and Satan (Revelation 12:9; 2 Corinthians 11:3). The Reformers (Calvin, Henry) stress that the essence of the first sin was unbelief flowering into pride and disobedience — the heart turned from trust to autonomy. That the man was “with her” (v.6) underscores his culpable passivity; the head appointed to guard the garden (Genesis 2:15) stood silent.
1.literary_structure: The temptation is built on a chiasm of God’s word twisted: the serpent’s “you will not surely die” (v.4) exactly inverts God’s “you shall surely die” (Genesis 2:17). The threefold appeal — “good for food… a delight to the eyes… to be desired to make one wise” (v.6) — is the template echoed in 1 John 2:16 (“the desires of the flesh and the desires of the eyes and pride of life”) and in the threefold wilderness temptation of Christ (Matthew 4:1–11). The opened eyes (v.7) are bitterly ironic: the promised enlightenment yields only the discovery of nakedness and the first frantic act of self-covering.
1.theological_application: This passage is the wellspring of the doctrine of the Fall and original sin. Sin enters not first as a deed but as misbelief — a refusal to take God at his word and a craving to define good and evil for oneself. The fig leaves (v.7) are humanity’s first religion of self-justification, the perennial attempt to cover guilt by our own works, which God will replace with garments he provides (v.21). The text warns that temptation still follows this grammar — questioning the goodness and truth of God’s commands — and calls believers to the obedience of faith, resisting the devil who “prowls around like a roaring lion” (1 Peter 5:8).
1.christology: The first Adam, tempted in a garden of plenty, fell; the last Adam (1 Corinthians 15:45), tempted in a barren wilderness, stood — meeting every satanic distortion with “It is written” (Matthew 4:4). Where Eve grasped at being “like God,” the eternal Son “did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped” but humbled himself (Philippians 2:6–8). The shame that drove our parents to hide is borne by Christ, who was stripped naked on the cross to clothe sinners in righteousness. The whole tragedy of the Fall is the dark backdrop against which the gospel’s remedy will shine.

8.synthesis: “They heard the sound of the LORD God walking in the garden in the cool of the day, and the man and his wife hid themselves” (v.8). God’s question, “Where are you?” (v.9), is not ignorance but summons — the seeking of a God who comes to the guilty. Adam confesses fear and nakedness but not sin (v.10); pressed, he blames the woman “whom you gave to be with me” (v.12) — and so, implicitly, God. The woman blames the serpent (v.13). The integrative thread is the fracture sin opens in every relationship at once: with God (they hide), within the self (shame), and between persons (blame) — yet God initiates the encounter, and his interrogation is the first movement of grace toward fallen humanity.
8.historical_context: The picture of the LORD “walking” in the garden depicts the intimate covenant fellowship now ruptured; the same divine presence that was blessing has become dread. Commentators in the Reformed tradition (Calvin, Keil and Delitzsch) note that God’s questions are forensic — a judicial inquiry conducted with mercy, giving opportunity for confession before sentence. Adam’s evasion, shifting blame to “the woman whom you gave” (v.12), is the first instance of the self-exculpation that has marked the human heart ever since, refusing the candor that leads to repentance.
8.literary_structure: The scene is a tightly ordered judgment inquest: God questions the man, the man deflects to the woman, the woman to the serpent — a descending chain that God will then answer in reverse, sentencing the serpent first, then the woman, then the man (vv.14–19). The repeated verb “hid” (v.8, v.10) frames the passage and signals the new condition of guilt. The poignant question “Where are you?” functions as the keynote — the whole of subsequent redemptive history is God’s answer to the hiddenness of the sinner.
8.theological_application: Sin’s immediate fruit is flight from God and the corrosion of human relationships into accusation. The passage exposes the futility of hiding from the omniscient God and the deeper futility of blame-shifting, which never reaches the honesty repentance requires. Yet the abiding comfort is that God seeks first: “Where are you?” precedes any cry from Adam. This is the pattern of grace — the shepherd who goes after the lost (Luke 15:4). Believers are called to come into the light and confess rather than excuse, knowing that the God who confronts also clothes (1 John 1:9).
8.christology: “Where are you?” anticipates the incarnation, when God would walk again among his people — “the Word became flesh and dwelt among us” (John 1:14) — to seek and to save the lost (Luke 19:10). Adam hid among the trees; Christ would be fastened to a tree, taking the curse of the guilty (Galatians 3:13). The blame Adam cast on the woman and on God is reversed at Calvary, where the sinless one accepts the blame he did not earn, so that those who hide may be found.

14.synthesis: God pronounces sentence: the serpent is cursed and, decisively, told, “I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and her offspring; he shall bruise your head, and you shall bruise his heel” (v.15). The woman receives pain in childbearing and a disordered marriage relation (v.16); the man, toil and thorns, “till you return to the ground… dust you are, and to dust you shall return” (vv.17–19). Yet Adam names his wife Eve, “the mother of all living” (v.20), an act of faith, and “the LORD God made for Adam and for his wife garments of skins and clothed them” (v.21). They are then expelled lest they eat of the tree of life, and cherubim guard the way (vv.22–24). The thread is judgment laced with mercy: real curse, real death — but a promise, a name of hope, and a God-given covering.
14.historical_context: The curses fall on the two great spheres of human vocation — the woman’s in fruitfulness and family, the man’s in work and the ground — reversing the blessings of Genesis 1:28. Thorns and thistles (v.18) make creation itself groan (Romans 8:20–22). The “garments of skins” (v.21) imply the first death in the created order, an animal slain to clothe sinners; many in the Reformed tradition (Henry, Gill) see here the seed of the sacrificial system — that sin’s shame is covered only at the cost of life. The expulsion eastward and the cherubim with the flaming sword (v.24) bar fallen humanity from unending life in its sinful state, an act of mercy as much as judgment.
14.literary_structure: Verse 15 is the famous protoevangelium, the “first gospel,” a fountainhead the rest of Scripture flows from — the seed of the woman against the seed of the serpent, head-crushing victory won through a wounded heel. The chiastic sentencing (serpent, woman, man) inverts the order of the temptation (serpent, woman, man eating). The naming of Eve (v.20) sits, strikingly, between sentence and clothing — a hinge of hope in the middle of judgment. “Dust you are, and to dust you shall return” (v.19) forms an inclusio with the dust from which man was formed (Genesis 2:7).
14.theological_application: Here the doctrines of the curse, death as sin’s wage (Romans 6:23), and the first promise of redemption are all set down together. Suffering, painful labor, marital strife, and mortality are traced to the Fall, not to God’s original design — a vital pastoral truth for those who suffer. Yet the same passage forbids despair: God clothes rather than abandons, and the protoevangelium guarantees that the serpent’s triumph is temporary. Believers live between the curse and its undoing, working cursed ground in hope, trusting the promised offspring.
14.christology: Genesis 3:15 is the Bible’s first proclamation of Christ: the offspring of the woman who crushes the serpent’s head is fulfilled in Jesus, “born of woman” (Galatians 4:4), who through death destroyed “the one who has the power of death, that is, the devil” (Hebrews 2:14), and under whose feet God will soon crush Satan (Romans 16:20). The garments of skin, won by a death, foreshadow the righteousness with which Christ clothes his people (Isaiah 61:10). The flaming sword that barred the tree of life is answered at the cross, where the way to life is reopened, so that the redeemed will eat at last from the tree of life in the new creation (Revelation 22:2, 14).
